title: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

reviews.summary: This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.
reviews.questions: none
reviews.limitations: The authors have stated the limitations properly. No potential negative societal impact.

Summary:
This paper combines the local prox update technique with the extrapolated cyclic algorithm and proposes the ECyclicPDA algorithm. While the local prox update technique is well understood, the authors reinterpret it as a coordinate method (CM). Then, the method is combined with a new extrapolated method. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can converge at a rate of O(1/T).

Strengths:
1. This paper presents a new understanding of the local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF. The combination of local OMD updates and extrapolated updates is original and interesting.
2. The theoretical results seem sound.
3. The paper is well-written.

Weaknesses:
1. It is hard to understand why we want to reinterpret the well-known local update rules of OMD with dilated DGF to CM. As we know, people already compute the strategy in a bottom-up fashion in previous work [1, 2, 3]. So, I think the main contribution is the combination of the local update method and the extrapolated cyclic method.
2. However, the ECyclicPDA algorithm seems less efficient than the traditional MP algorithm. For every infoset, an extra traversal of the subgame rooted at the infoset is needed to compute the “extrapolated” vector. This could be infeasible for large-scale games.
3. The experimental results show that the proposed ECyclicPDA performs better than MP. The results are not surprising when considering that ECyclicPDA can be very time-consuming.
4. The algorithm does not compare with related optimistic OMD [4] algorithms for EFGs.


[1] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Optimistic regret minimization for extensive-form games via dilated distance-generating functions." Advances in neural information processing systems 32 (2019).
[2] Farina, Gabriele, Christian Kroer, and Tuomas Sandholm. "Better regularization for sequential decision spaces: Fast convergence rates for Nash, correlated, and team equilibria." arXiv preprint arXiv:2105.12954 (2021).
[3] Liu, Weiming, et al. "Equivalence analysis between counterfactual regret minimization and online mirror descent." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2022.
[4] Lee, Chung-Wei, Christian Kroer, and Haipeng Luo. "Last-iterate convergence in extensive-form games." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 34 (2021): 14293-14305.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a novel algorithm for solving extensive‐form games by reinterpreting local optimistic mirror descent (OMD) updates—built on dilated distance‐generating functions (DGFs)—as a coordinate‐descent method, and coupling these updates with a new extrapolated cyclic traversal scheme. The authors prove that ECyclicPDA achieves an ergodic convergence rate of O(1/T), and they demonstrate in experiments that it outperforms classical mirror-prox in head-to-head comparisons.

Strengths  
1. Conceptual novelty: The paper offers an original viewpoint by casting local OMD updates with dilated DGFs into a coordinate-method framework.  
2. Algorithmic design: The integration of these coordinate-style updates with an extrapolated cyclic scheme is creative and well motivated from a theoretical standpoint.  
3. Rigorous analysis: The convergence proof establishing the O(1/T) rate is sound and carefully presented.  
4. Clarity of exposition: The manuscript is well organized and clearly written; definitions, algorithmic steps, and proof sketches are all accessible to readers familiar with game‐theoretic optimization.

Weaknesses  
1. Practical motivation: It remains unclear why one should reinterpret the standard local OMD prox‐rules as a coordinate method, especially in light of existing bottom‐up or hierarchical approaches to dilated mirror descent.  
2. Computational overhead: ECyclicPDA requires an extra traversal of each subgame at every information set to compute the extrapolated vector. This additional pass may render the method less efficient than traditional mirror‐prox in large‐scale settings.  
3. Experimental scope: The empirical evaluation compares ECyclicPDA only against mirror prox. In the broader literature on optimistic OMD for extensive‐form games, several variants have been proposed; omitting these baselines leaves open the question of whether the observed gains stem from algorithmic improvements or simply from heavier computational effort.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Please elaborate on the practical scenarios or problem regimes where treating local dilated‐DGF OMD updates as a coordinate method leads to genuine advantages over standard approaches. In particular, discussing memory usage or parallelization benefits could strengthen the motivation.  
– To support claims of empirical superiority, include experiments against related optimistic‐OMD algorithms tailored to extensive‐form games (for example, optimistic CFR or other dilated‐DGF OMD variants). This broader comparison would clarify whether ECyclicPDA’s performance edge holds when matched for computational effort.

Recommendation: Weak Accept (6)  
Overall, ECyclicPDA presents a technically solid contribution with a clean theoretical guarantee and an inventive algorithmic twist. While questions remain about its practical efficiency and benchmarking, these are addressable in a revision. I therefore recommend a weak accept.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+
reviews.strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).
reviews.weaknesses: As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?
reviews.questions: Please comment on the questions about resetting.
reviews.limitations: Adequate

Summary:
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a new first order method for solving extensive form games.  The main idea is to implement something in the spirit of coordinate descent where improving directions can be found by considering a part of the current iterate in isolation.  Empirical results show it generally outperforms other first-order methods and approaches the performance of CFR+.  Additionally, a heuristic to restart the process is introduced which improves the performance of both ECyclicPDA and CFR+

Strengths:
ECyclicPDA is a nice contribution that takes the growing collection of FOMs for EFGs in a new direction.  The analysis seems to involve non-trivial technical innovations.  The empirical results are convining and show continued progress toward cloasing the gap with CFR-based approaches.

The restarting heuristic is also very interesting, and the ability to get performance improvements with CFR+ is particularly nice (and intuitive in hindsight).

Weaknesses:
As far as I can tell, the details of how restarting is implemented are never clearly explained.  The clearest explanation I can find is in the introduction on lines 88-91, but even taking that as the full specification, I don’t see where the tuning of the parameter about when to restart is specified.  It would also be nice to have a bit of explanation / intuition for how restarting is benefiting various algorithms.  For CFR+ I can see how resetting the regret sums and the averaging process could be beneficial.  For FOMs I’m less clear why it is useful.  Is it purely from resetting the averaging process?

Questions:
Please comment on the questions about resetting.

Limitations:
Adequate

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents ECyclicPDA, a novel first‐order method for solving two‐player zero‐sum extensive‐form games that operates in a coordinate‐descent‐style manner. The authors develop several non‐trivial technical tools to analyze the algorithm’s convergence and demonstrate through experiments that ECyclicPDA consistently outperforms existing first‐order methods, narrowing the practical performance gap with the state‐of‐the‐art CFR+ algorithm. In addition, the paper introduces a simple restarting heuristic which not only accelerates ECyclicPDA but also yields improved convergence for CFR+. Overall, the work is well motivated, technically solid, and empirically compelling.

Strengths  
1. Novel Algorithmic Direction: ECyclicPDA represents a fresh approach to first‐order optimization in extensive‐form games, moving beyond standard extragradient and mirror‐prox techniques.  
2. Technical Contributions: The convergence analysis involves intricate arguments adapted to the extensive‐form setting, showcasing genuine innovation in algorithm design and proof techniques.  
3. Empirical Performance: Comprehensive experiments on benchmark poker and game‐tree domains convincingly demonstrate that ECyclicPDA outperforms competing first‐order methods and approaches CFR+ performance in both speed and solution quality.  
4. Restarting Heuristic: The proposed restart mechanism is elegant in its simplicity and, according to the results, provides consistent speed‐ups not only for ECyclicPDA but also when applied to CFR+. This cross‐algorithm benefit underlines the heuristic’s practical value.

Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement  
While the core contributions are strong, the paper currently leaves some important practical and conceptual questions about the restarting heuristic unanswered:

1. Lack of Implementation Details  
   • The description in lines 88–91 indicates that both regret sums and the averaging process are reset upon restart, but it stops short of a complete, step‐by‐step specification. It is unclear whether the full dual variables or only a subset are reset in the FOM context.  

2. Unspecified Tuning Criterion  
   • The manuscript does not specify the exact condition or metric used to trigger a restart in experiments. Is it based on a fixed iteration count, a threshold on exploitability, or another convergence test?  

3. Missing Intuitive/ Theoretical Justification  
   • Beyond empirical gains, readers would benefit from a discussion—if not a formal argument—explaining why restarting should improve convergence for both ECyclicPDA and CFR+. What properties of the dynamics or the game tree make restarts effective?  

Concrete Suggestions  
1. Full Algorithmic Description of Restarts  
   • In the main text or an appendix, provide pseudocode or a detailed bullet‐point list that clarifies exactly which state variables are reset and how the averaging windows are reinitialized.  

2. Clarify Restart Trigger and Parameter Tuning  
   • Report in the experimental section the precise criterion for initiating restarts (e.g., “we restart every T iterations” or “when the gradient norm falls below ε”). Include a brief discussion on how T or ε were selected or validated.  

3. Add Intuitive/Theoretical Insight  
   • Even if a full proof is beyond the paper’s scope, include a short paragraph hypothesizing why restarts combat drift or stale averages in both FOMs and CFR+, perhaps relating to breaking cyclic behaviors in coordinate updates.  

Recommendation: Accept (Rating = 7)  
This work offers a significant step forward in first‐order methods for extensive‐form games, combining solid theoretical analysis with strong empirical results. The restarting heuristic further enhances practical impact. I recommend acceptance, contingent on the authors addressing the clarification and exposition requests regarding the restart mechanism.

reviews.summary: The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.
reviews.strengths: The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.
reviews.weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.
reviews.questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.
reviews.limitations: Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “A Block-Coordinate Extrapolated Mirror-Prox Method for Extensive-Form Games”

Summary  
This paper presents an application of the CODER extrapolation framework to two-player extensive-form games (EFGs) with L₁‐proximal setup, leveraging the treeplex structure to enable linear-time block-coordinate updates. The authors prove an O(1/T) convergence rate independent of the total number of variables and demonstrate through experiments (with a simple restarting heuristic) that their method can match the empirical performance of CFR+ on benchmark games.

Strengths  
• Clever specialization of CODER to EFGs: By exploiting the inherent treeplex decomposition, the authors achieve efficient block-coordinate mirror-prox updates in time linear in the game size.  
• Sharp convergence bound: The O(1/T) rate is established without explicit dependence on the dimension, which is appealing in large game instances.  
• L₁-norm extrapolation validated: The paper shows that CODER’s extrapolated mirror-prox, when instantiated with an L₁ prox on the sequence form simplex, retains its convergence guarantees.  
• Empirical promise with restarting: A simple restarting heuristic leads to performance on par with CFR+ in several domains, suggesting practical viability.

Weaknesses  
• Novelty of the block extension: The original CODER framework already accommodates blockwise updates, so this work’s main algorithmic advance amounts to a routine generalization from scalar to block updates, specialized to a trivially separable two‐player treeplex.  
• Limited theoretical insight: The independence of the rate from the number of variables follows directly from having a constant number of blocks, making the result unsurprising in hindsight.  
• Heuristic‐only improvements: Without restarting, the method trails CFR+ significantly, and the restarting strategy—while effective—lacks any theoretical backing.  
• Comparative claims: Even with restarting, the new method only reaches comparable, not superior, performance to CFR+.  
• Possible experimental bias: Step‐size tuning appears to have been applied solely to the proposed algorithm, raising concerns about fairness in the mirror‐prox baseline comparisons.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Fairness of Baseline Tuning  
   • To ensure an equitable experimental comparison, apply the same step‐size tuning or a constant step‐size protocol to baseline methods such as Mirror-Prox. This will clarify whether observed gains stem from the extrapolation framework or simply from more aggressive parameter selection.  

2. Investigation of Performance Curves  
   • The abrupt vertical drops seen in the evaluation plots are puzzling. Please verify whether these stem from numerical instability, mis‐computed optima, or reporting artifacts, and provide an explanation or corrected figures.  

3. Clarity of Algorithm Presentation  
   • Algorithm 1 is presented with dense notation. I recommend adding parentheses to clarify the order of operations and explicitly annotating which variables (e.g., prox‐centers, momentum terms) are carried forward between iterations. This will make the method easier to follow and implement.

4. Theoretical Context  
   • Since the paper’s convergence proof largely mirrors existing CODER analyses, it may be helpful to more explicitly contrast what, if any, new challenges the EFG setting introduces. If separability is truly trivial in two-player treeplexes, a brief discussion acknowledging this would set readers’ expectations.  

5. Scope of Results  
   • The restarting heuristic clearly boosts practical performance, but readers would benefit from any partial theoretical insight (even conjectural) into why and when restarting is effective. A short discussion—even if speculative—would strengthen the narrative.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This work delivers a technically solid adaptation of the CODER framework to extensive-form games, accompanied by a rigorous O(1/T) guarantee and promising empirical results under restarting. At the same time, the theoretical novelty is incremental, and the experimental section would benefit from a more balanced comparison and clearer algorithmic exposition. Despite these reservations, the paper offers valuable insights into block-coordinate extrapolation in large-scale EFGs and is likely to interest researchers working on game-theoretic optimization. I therefore recommend a Weak Accept (Rating: 6).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The draft considers solving the extended-form game (EFG) with extrapolated block-coordinate descent methods, proving that it achieves O(1/T) convergence. The authors further show that with a restarting strategy, the proposed algorithm may be comparable sometimes to state-of-the-art algorithms like CFR+. In my understanding, the proposed algorithm is essentially a generalization of the CODER algorithm proposed by Song [37] in a way from coordinate descent (CD) to blockwise CD and specializes the domain to be linear functional and treeplex. I.e., the algorithm is essentially the same (line-by-line corresponded) as CODER but generalizes the scalar coordinate to blocks of "separable" variables.  For the specialization, the authors focus on the context of EFG with bilinear score function and 2-player treeplex (linear probability simplex), where the prox is taken on L1-norm instead of the L2-norm by CODER. Implementation-wise, the proposed algorithm uses the recursive structure of the treeplex for efficient linear time computation.

Strengths:
The major contribution of the draft is that it provides an instance to efficiently implement the CODER algorithm on EFG with blockwise L1 proximal and shows that with restarting, such implementation may be comparable with the state-of-the-art algorithm like CFR+. It also analyzes the scenario and provides an O(1/T) convergence rate without dependence on the number of variables. To summarize, the draft shows that a simple CODER extrapolation works well when taking L1 specialization on EFG.

Weaknesses:
However, I would like to claim that the original CODER method may already cover the blockwise updates. I.e., if you look into the proof of CODER, it generally does not use the scalar property of the proximal and treats variables as blocks. They are using the $d$-dimension notation for simplicity and already claiming they are doing block CD in their abstract. In the EFG case, the d=2, so naturally (if the proximal works), the convergence complexity doesn't depend on the number of variables since blocks=2 is a constant. So the draft's theoretical result (independence on the number of variables) is not surprising. And the proposed method does not relax the "separability assumption" since the 2-player treeplex is separable as two blocks. The major difference/contribution here is that it performs the analysis on the L1 proximal and does the analysis.

Further, although it's good to see that the simple method works with restarting, the restarting part doesn't have theoretical support. I.e., without restarting, the theoretically supported method is not comparable to the state-of-the-art. And with restarting, the "full" method is comparable but not really much better than CFR+. Thus these factors put the paper in a borderline condition. However, the analysis seems solid, and I would give a borderline acceptance.

Questions:
1. (Figure 2) Only doing the step-size optimization for your method and not the baseline is tricky for the experiment. For fairness, it should be either all constant step-size or all step-size tuned for the baseline. Otherwise, the reader cannot be sure whether the proposed method is better than MP.
2. (Figure 4) The vertical drop in the curves looks like either a numerical issue or a wrong optimal value.
3. (Algorithm 1) Adding parenthesis and denoting what you maintain in the computation may be good for clarity.

Limitations:
Not applicable since the paper does not have a limitation section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper develops a cyclic block-coordinate-descent-like method for two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFG). Such methods for EFG are  difficult due to non-separable nature of block structure of the problem. The decision problem for a player in a EFG can be formulated using Treeplex, for which regularizing functions can be constructed through the framework of dilated regularizers. These dilated regularizing functions allow recursive prox computations. This paper utilizes this frame work to develop an extrapolated cyclic algorithm to perform pseudo-block updates.  They demonstrate O(1/T) convergence rate for two-player zero-sum Nash equilibrium  using this method, and provide a specific algorithmic implementation which shows that runtime of the proposed method  is independent of the number of blocks. 

Experimental evaluation on EFG benchmark games is performed using three different dilated regularizers -dilated entropy, dilated global entropy, and dilated $\ell_2$ with different block construction strategies. Experiments demonstrate the benefit of using blocks over non-block based approach in  some games, and no significant diffference between different block construction methods in others.  The results show improved  performance over the state of the art first order method (Mirror-Prox). Further, they introduce a restarting heuristic which speeds up the proposed method as well as the baselines.
reviews.strengths:
The paper is generally well written, describing necessary background to help readers understand the paper.

The proposed algorithm seems novel, though I must admit I am not familiar with the literature.

The experimental evaluation seems convincing, the proposed method outperforms SOTA FOM
reviews.weaknesses:
line 105-``As discussed before RCMs are not applicable to our setting''. It is not discussed anywhere in the paper why randomized coordinate methods are not applicable.

The paper could include a discussion in the related work about [i]  which develops primal-dual coordinate methods for solving bilinear saddle-point problems.

[i] Carmon et al. Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games
reviews.limitations: The authors discuss open question of why restarting work with regularizers  other than $\ell_2$. The authors adequately addressed the limitations.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces the first cyclic block-coordinate-descent–style algorithm for solving two‐player zero‐sum extensive‐form games in sequence‐form. By combining dilated regularizers with an extrapolation mechanism, the authors overcome the non‐separability of sequence‐form polytopes and obtain pseudo‐block updates that guarantee an ergodic convergence rate of O(1/T). Crucially, the runtime per iteration does not grow with the number of blocks. The paper also proposes an adaptive restarting heuristic that accelerates convergence not only for the new method but for existing algorithms as well. Empirical results on standard extensive‐form benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains over Mirror‐Prox and competitive performance compared to CFR+ variants. Overall, the presentation is clear, the theoretical analysis is sound, and the experiments are thorough.

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is very well written. The background on extensive‐form games and first‐order methods is comprehensive, making the technical contributions accessible.  
2. Novel Algorithmic Contribution: Addressing non‐separable constraints in sequence‐form strategy polytopes via cyclic coordinate updates and dilated regularizers is an original idea that fills a gap in the literature.  
3. Strong Theoretical Guarantees: The authors prove an O(1/T) ergodic convergence rate and show that the per‐iteration cost remains independent of the block count—an attractive property for large games.  
4. Convincing Empirical Evaluation: Experiments on several established benchmarks show that the proposed method outperforms Mirror‐Prox and, with the restarting heuristic, is competitive with high-performance CFR+ variants. The ablation over different regularizers and block constructions is also informative.  

Weaknesses  
1. Applicability of Randomized Coordinate Methods: The paper asserts (Line 105) that randomized coordinate methods (RCMs) are not suitable for the extensive‐form setting, but it does not explain the specific obstacles or failure modes that arise.  
2. Omission in Related Work: There is no discussion of Carmon et al.’s recent primal-dual coordinate methods for matrix games (“Coordinate Methods for Matrix Games”), which tackle bilinear saddle-point problems using coordinate updates. A comparison or at least a contextual discussion would be valuable.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Include a brief but concrete discussion—either in the main text or as a remark—outlining why standard randomized coordinate schemes break down when applied to sequence-form polytopes. For example, the authors could highlight challenges in maintaining feasibility or bounding the bias introduced by random block selection.  
2. Expand the related‐work section to mention Carmon et al. (2020) and clarify how their primal-dual coordinate approach for matrix games relates to (or differs from) the cyclic updates and dilated regularizers proposed in this paper. This will help readers situate the new method within the broader coordinate‐method literature.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This work makes a significant technical contribution by developing the first cyclic block‐coordinate‐descent approach tailored to extensive‐form games, complete with strong convergence guarantees and practical speedups via restarting. The theoretical novelty is well supported by a thorough experimental evaluation. After addressing the minor issues above—namely, explaining the inapplicability of RCMs and situating the method relative to Carmon et al.—this paper will be an excellent addition to the literature. I therefore recommend acceptance.  

Rating: 7 (Accept)

reviews.summary: This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.
reviews.strengths: To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.
reviews.weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.
reviews.questions: no
reviews.limitations: I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

Summary:
This work proposes a cyclic coordinate descent method to solve the two-player zero-sum extended form game (EFG) and derives the convergence.

Strengths:
To me, solving problems with non-separable constraints by coordinate-descent-type methods is novel and interesting. Therefore, I believe that this is indeed a contribution.

Weaknesses:
I'm not familiar with this area so please forgave me if I made some mistakes.

1. Possibly expensive computational cost per iteration. Following the literature survey in the manuscript, I read reference [1] which studies coordinate descent for solving optimization problems with non-separable non-smooth objective functions. I found that in both the algorithm in [1] and the algorithm in this paper, although only partial gradient is needed at each update, the proximal step with respect to the whole nonsmooth function is needed. More specifically, in Lines 8 and 11, the argmin step might be difficult and computationally expensive since the argmin step is over the whole X and Y and involve all the blocks.

2.  This concern follows the first one but focuses on the experiments: the comparison with non-coordinate algorithms may be unfair. The current comparison is in terms of the number of full gradient computations. However, gradient computation is not the only computation cost, Lines 8 and 11 also cause computation costs. I would suggest a comparison in terms of wall clock time.

Questions:
no

Limitations:
I do not see any potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Review for [Paper Title]

Summary  
This paper proposes a novel cyclic coordinate descent algorithm for solving non-separable extensive-form games. By extending coordinate-descent–type methods to handle coupled constraints arising in extensive-form formulations, the authors address an important gap in the literature. Empirical results demonstrate promising convergence behavior when measured in terms of full gradient computations.

Strengths  
- The core idea of applying coordinate-descent–style updates to extensive-form games with non-separable constraints is both original and technically interesting.  
- The algorithmic framework is well motivated, and the mathematical development—particularly the convergence analysis—appears sound and rigorous.  

Weaknesses  
- Although the method leverages partial gradients in each coordinate update, the algorithm’s per-iteration cost remains substantial because the proximal (argmin) subroutines in Lines 8 and 11 operate over the entire decision domains X and Y. These global proximal steps involve all blocks rather than only local coordinates, potentially negating the efficiency gains typically expected from coordinate methods.  
- In the experimental evaluation, running time is quantified solely by the number of full gradient computations. However, this metric omits the cost of calling the proximal oracle, which can be significant in the non-separable setting. As a result, the comparisons to baseline methods may not fully reflect the true computational burden of the proposed approach.

Suggestions for Improvement  
- To provide a more balanced assessment of practical efficiency, I recommend reporting wall-clock timings for all algorithms under consideration. Including end-to-end runtimes that capture both gradient and proximal computations will clarify whether the theoretical advantages translate into real-world speedups.  
- It may also be helpful to profile or estimate the cost of each argmin subproblem versus gradient evaluation, so readers can better understand the trade-offs involved in different problem scales and structures.

Detailed Comments  
- Technical Detail (Lines 8 and 11): The reader should be made explicitly aware that the proximal update solves a joint optimization over the full nonsmooth function on X (resp. Y). Emphasizing the complexity of these steps will highlight why per-iteration costs might be higher than in separable-coordinate schemes.  
- Experimental Fairness: Since the proximal oracle may dominate runtime, simply counting full gradients risks underestimating the real expense. Reporting wall-clock times or an equivalent composite cost measure will strengthen the empirical claims.

Decision  
Overall, this work makes a valuable contribution by bridging coordinate-descent methods and extensive-form game optimization. While the computational cost per iteration and the evaluation methodology warrant further scrutiny, these concerns do not outweigh the paper’s novelty and theoretical soundness. I therefore recommend a rating of 5: Borderline Accept.

reviews.summary: The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.
reviews.strengths: In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.
reviews.weaknesses: The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.
reviews.questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?
reviews.limitations: During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces ECyclicPDA, a cyclic coordinate-descent-style first-order method for extensive-form games formulated over the sequence-form polytope. The key novelty lies in using dilated regularizers to enable pseudo block-wise proximal updates, along with a simple restarting heuristic that accelerates convergence. The authors provide theoretical runtime guarantees that are independent of the number of blocks, and they position their approach relative to existing coordinate-descent and first-order algorithms. Empirical results show that ECyclicPDA matches or exceeds the performance of CFR+ on the Battleship benchmark, suggesting first-order methods can be competitive on non-trivial extensive-form games.

Strengths  
• Novel algorithmic framework: ECyclicPDA offers a fresh coordinate-descent-style approach tailored to large sequential games.  
• Dilated regularizers: The paper leverages strongly convex, dilated regularizers to support pseudo block-wise proximal updates, which is an interesting technical contribution.  
• Restarting heuristic: The proposed restart scheme is simple yet effective, and it can be applied to ECyclicPDA as well as other solvers.  
• Clear positioning: The authors situate their work well against coordinate-descent and first-order methods, highlighting where ECyclicPDA stands relative to mirror-prox and CFR+.

Weaknesses  
• Limited runtime analysis: While the paper claims that runtime does not depend on the number of blocks, there is a lack of detailed empirical timing comparisons against state-of-the-art methods such as mirror-prox or DCFR.  
• Missing competitive baselines: The DCFR algorithm—one of the strongest first-order/variant baselines—is neither discussed nor evaluated, leaving the experimental picture incomplete.  
• Narrow evaluation: In the experiments, ECyclicPDA only clearly outperforms CFR+ on the Battleship game. It remains unclear why this advantage arises and whether it generalizes to other benchmarks.  
• Assumption clarity: The “nice” property required for strongly convex dilated regularizers is stated but not fully justified or illustrated in practice.  
• Appendix issues: Several figures in the appendix share identical captions without specifying which averaging scheme (linear, quadratic, or uniform) is depicted.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Provide a thorough runtime comparison, including execution time and memory requirements, against mirror-prox, CFR+, DCFR, and any other leading methods.  
2. Incorporate DCFR (and possibly other recent variants) into both the discussion and the experimental evaluation to give a more complete assessment of competitive alternatives.  
3. Clarify and justify the “nice” assumption on dilated regularizers, perhaps by giving examples of how the gradients and prox oracles can be computed efficiently.  
4. Correct the appendix figure captions to clearly indicate which averaging scheme is used in each plot.  
5. Investigate and explain the specific factors causing ECyclicPDA to outperform CFR+ on the Battleship benchmark, and consider adding experiments on additional games to test generality.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, ECyclicPDA is a technically sound and innovative contribution to the literature on first-order methods for extensive-form games. Although the empirical evaluation and runtime analysis need to be strengthened, the algorithmic ideas and theoretical insights are of sufficient merit to warrant publication. I therefore recommend a borderline accept.  

Rating: 5: Borderline Accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Reviewer Summary  
This paper introduces a novel block-coordinate optimization method for solving extensive-form games. The authors establish a favorable theoretical convergence rate and provide experimental validation of their block-construction strategy on four benchmark games. However, empirical results indicate that the proposed approach often underperforms PCFR+ in terms of convergence, and key experimental details—such as wall-clock runtimes, variance estimates, and an explicit statement of contributions—are missing. After the authors’ rebuttal, which satisfactorily addressed some concerns, I believe the technical merits warrant a borderline acceptance (Rating: 5).

Strengths  
1. Clarity and Presentation: The manuscript is exceptionally well written with no discernible grammar or stylistic issues, and the introduction remains accessible even to readers less familiar with extensive-form game solvers.  
2. Theoretical Contributions: The paper offers a rigorous convergence analysis for the block-coordinate method, demonstrating a provable rate that compares favorably to existing theory.  
3. Experimental Validation of Block Construction: The authors carefully justify their choice of block partitioning through empirical ablation studies.  
4. Thorough Benchmarking Setup: Performance is evaluated via the primal-dual gap on four established benchmark games, with comparisons drawn against PCFR+, mirror prox, and other solvers (Figures 2 and 4).

Weaknesses  
1. Empirical Performance Lag: In Figures 2 and 4, the proposed method consistently converges more slowly than PCFR+, in some cases by a significant margin.  
2. Missing Wall-Clock Runtimes: All performance metrics are reported in iteration counts or gap values; actual runtime measurements (seconds or minutes) are omitted.  
3. Lack of Error Bars: None of the experimental plots include error bars or confidence intervals, making it difficult to assess variability across multiple runs.  
4. Implicit Contribution Statements: The paper explains its innovations indirectly within the narrative rather than presenting a concise, numbered list of contributions.  
5. Superficial Limitations Discussion: The limitations section primarily notes an incomplete understanding of when certain game structures favor the proposed method, without deeper analysis.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• Report Wall-Clock Time: To provide a more practical performance picture, please include empirical runtime comparisons in actual time units (e.g., seconds or minutes) for all algorithms.  
• Add Error Bars: Incorporate variance estimates—error bars or shaded confidence regions—into every convergence plot to convey statistical significance.  
• Explicit Contributions List: At the end of the introduction, insert a compact, numbered summary of the paper’s key contributions to improve readability and transparency.  
• Deepen the Limitations Section: Expand the discussion on how specific game structures (e.g., depth, branching factor) impact performance. If possible, include empirical or theoretical insights illustrating these effects.  
• Time-Complexity Clarification: Although the rebuttal addresses some runtime concerns, it would be beneficial to supplement the theoretical convergence rate with a time-based complexity statement or at least a discussion linking iteration counts to expected run times.

Technical Details  
– Benchmarking on four games with primal-dual gap as the performance metric.  
– Comparative evaluation against PCFR+, mirror prox, and other solvers (see Figures 2 and 4).  
– Theoretical guarantee on convergence rate, but no explicit mapping to wall-clock time complexity.  
– Absence of error bars in reported results.  
– Post-rebuttal, the authors provided additional justification for runtime overhead but still lack concrete timing data.

Final Recommendation  
Overall, the paper makes a solid theoretical and methodological contribution to extensive-form game solving. The writing quality and the rigorous convergence analysis are commendable, but the empirical evaluation requires more thorough reporting and discussion. On balance, I recommend a borderline acceptance (Rating: 5). With the suggested revisions—especially the inclusion of runtime measurements, variance estimates, explicit contributions, and a richer limitations analysis—the work will significantly strengthen its impact and clarity.

title: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM System

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a neural implicit representation based collaborative SLAM technique. They introduce a learnable feature encoding for each point in the 3d scene based on neural rendering subsequently using it for depth and color map estimation. They introduce a centralized learning strategy to share MLPs and learn better weights. They also introduce a LM algorithm based global pose optimization and key frame centric point cloud adjustment.
reviews.strengths:
The federated learning strategy improves the neural rendering performance. Map refinement based on keyframe centric neural point field.
By encoding scene geometry and appearance with 3D points, when camera poses are optimized, these features are easily adjusted as well.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The paper only evaluates on one synthetic datasets. It would be nice to see evaluations on more datasets 
2. Better and more robust neural rendering techniques could have been used to improve the radiance, depth and color rendering
reviews.questions:
1. The paper is not clear whether it includes neural field generation time in tracking/mapping time while comparing with Nice SLAM. 
2. The paper lacks citations. For example, in the mapping and tracking section, a similar technique is used in Nice SLAM and I see no mention of it.
reviews.limitations:
1.The paper does produce SOTA results on some of the scenes of the dataset chosen. No explanation is given to why it doesn't produce better results on the rest. 
2. The paper is not novel enough. It rather combines the best of many other works and produces good results.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a collaborative SLAM framework built upon a neural implicit representation where each 3D point carries learnable features for geometry and appearance. The system employs a two-stage (distributed-to-centralized) or “federated” MLP learning strategy, shares and fine-tunes network weights across agents, and integrates a Levenberg–Marquardt–based global pose graph optimization to enforce loop closures. After optimization, keyframe-centric point cloud adjustment refines the map in world coordinates. The authors report state-of-the-art tracking, mapping, and rendering performance on a synthetic dataset.

Strengths  
1. Federated Learning Strategy: By first training per-sequence MLPs and then aggregating through federated averaging and fine-tuning, the method demonstrably improves neural rendering quality—both in radiance estimation and depth consistency—compared to naive single-agent training.  
2. Keyframe-Centric Map Refinement: Associating each 3D point with its originating keyframe allows straightforward, world-centric adjustment of features after pose updates. This design elegantly handles feature consistency following loop closure corrections.  
3. Consistent Geometry & Appearance Encoding: The per-point learnable feature representation, combined with neural rendering, facilitates a unified treatment of geometry and color, which remains stable even after pose graph updates.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Limited Evaluation Scope: Experiments are confined to a single synthetic dataset. I recommend evaluating the approach on additional real-world and synthetic benchmarks to validate generality and robustness.  
2. Rendering Robustness: While the federated strategy helps, the paper’s neural rendering still shows room for improvement in radiance, depth, and color fidelity. Incorporating or at least comparing to more advanced neural rendering modules could strengthen the technical contribution.  
3. Runtime Breakdown Ambiguity: It is unclear whether the reported tracking and mapping runtimes include the cost of neural field generation. Please clarify this aspect and, if needed, provide a detailed runtime breakdown, especially in comparison to related systems such as NICE-SLAM.  
4. Explanation of Scene-Dependent Performance: The manuscript reports state-of-the-art results on some scenes but lower performance on others without analysis. An ablation or discussion explaining these discrepancies would help readers understand the method’s strengths and limitations.  
5. Novelty and References: Although the combination of federated learning, implicit neural representations, and pose graph optimization is technically solid, the novelty appears incremental. Emphasizing any unique algorithmic insights beyond integrating existing components would improve the paper’s impact. Additionally, the mapping and tracking pipeline closely resembles NICE-SLAM—please add explicit citations and discuss differences.  

Minor Comments  
• In the mapping and tracking sections, cite NICE-SLAM and related neural SLAM works.  
• Clearly state in the experimental section whether neural field generation time is included in reported runtimes.  
• Highlight any optimization or architectural tweaks that distinguish your federated averaging scheme from standard approaches.

Conclusion  
This work presents a technically sound collaborative SLAM framework that cleverly integrates federated MLP learning, Levenberg–Marquardt pose graph optimization, and keyframe-centric map refinement. However, its evaluation is currently too narrow and the presentation would benefit from clearer runtime accounting, deeper analysis of scene-dependent behavior, and stronger positioning relative to prior art. With these improvements, the paper has the potential for significant impact. I therefore recommend a rating of 6: Weak Accept.

reviews.summary: The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.
reviews.strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.
reviews.questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

Strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.

Questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

Limitations:
The limitations have been discussed in the conclusion section.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural SLAM framework that unifies front-end odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global pose graph refinement within a novel neural point–based 3D scene representation. By associating neural points with keyframes and employing a two-stage distributed-to-centralized learning strategy, the system enables multi-agent consistency and cooperation. Comprehensive experiments on Replica indoor RGB-D datasets demonstrate that CP-SLAM outperforms both state-of-the-art neural implicit and traditional SLAM baselines in localization accuracy and reconstruction quality. The authors also include detailed ablation studies and full implementation specifications to facilitate reproducibility.

Strengths  
1. Novel Representation. The core contribution is a keyframe-associated neural point cloud that supports dense collaborative mapping and straightforward fusion after pose optimization.  
2. End-to-End SLAM Pipeline. CP-SLAM retains complete front-end and back-end modules—odometry estimation, loop detection, sub-map fusion (with grid-based non-maximum suppression), and Levenberg–Marquardt–based global pose graph optimization—within a unified neural framework.  
3. Versatile Evaluation. The method is tested in both single-agent and two-agent modes, showing consistent improvements over recent neural RGB-D SLAM approaches and classic SLAM systems.  
4. State-of-the-Art Performance. On Replica indoor sequences, CP-SLAM achieves superior tracking accuracy and reconstruction fidelity compared to baselines such as NICE-SLAM and DROID-SLAM (where applicable), validating the effectiveness of its pose graph optimization, point-based representation, and sampling concentration.  
5. Ablation Studies. The paper provides thorough ablations that isolate the contributions of global refinement, neural point representation, and the sampling strategy, confirming the necessity of each component.  
6. Reproducibility. Detailed implementation parameters (GPU specifications, training regimes, rendering-based optimization settings, post-optimization fine-tuning) are clearly documented.

Weaknesses  
1. High Computational Demand. CP-SLAM’s reliance on GPU acceleration to process multiple RGB-D streams concurrently may hinder deployment on resource-constrained platforms.  
2. Loop-Closure Sensitivity. The rendering-based optimization for loop-closure relative pose estimation can become inaccurate under large viewpoint changes, potentially introducing drift during sub-map fusion.  
3. Limited Comparative Scope. The two-agent experiments benchmark only against traditional SLAM systems, without direct comparison to other collaborative neural SLAM methods, leaving the relative multi-agent performance somewhat unclear.  
4. Scalability Unaddressed. No large-scale multi-agent scenarios are evaluated, so the framework’s behavior under increasing numbers of agents or dataset complexity remains undemonstrated.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Scalability Analysis. Clarify how CP-SLAM scales with dataset size and agent count, and consider reporting runtime and memory usage trends as the environment grows.  
• Real-World Validation. Evaluate the method on real-world RGB-D datasets to establish practical applicability beyond synthetic Replica scenes.  
• Implementation Insights. Discuss any challenges encountered during deployment (e.g., synchronization overhead, memory bottlenecks) to guide future users.  
• Additional Baselines. Include comparisons with other leading neural SLAM systems (e.g., NICE-SLAM, DROID-SLAM) in both single- and multi-agent settings to fully contextualize improvements.  
• World-Centric Map Adjustment. Offer a clearer explanation of why the world-centric point cloud representation cannot be readily adjusted post-optimization without the keyframe association mechanism.

Conclusion  
CP-SLAM presents a technically robust and well-engineered collaborative SLAM solution, introducing a compelling neural point–based representation and delivering strong empirical results. While its high GPU requirements and loop-closure sensitivity merit further investigation, the paper makes a solid contribution to neural SLAM research. I recommend a weak accept.  

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

reviews.summary: The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.
reviews.strengths: The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.
reviews.weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.
reviews.questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.
reviews.limitations: One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.
