reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

After rebuttal: The authors addressed all of my questions. Thank you.

Limitations:
One of the main limitations of framerate/view point change is addressed by the authors.. an initial experiment to showcase the effect would have been nice though.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “Collaborative RGB-D SLAM with Implicit Point-Based Mapping and Federated MLP Averaging”

Summary  
This paper presents a collaborative RGB-D SLAM system built around a point-based implicit field (inspired by Point-NeRF) coupled with a standard front end/back end for pose graph optimization. Each agent builds a local implicit map, and learned NetVLAD descriptors detect loop closures to align sub-maps. After pose graph fusion, a federated learning strategy averages and refines the distributed MLP weights to yield a globally consistent implicit scene representation. Experiments on synthetic indoor sequences demonstrate substantial gains in both tracking accuracy and reconstruction quality compared to existing methods.

Strengths  
• The use of discrete point-based features provides an effective interface between the implicit NeRF-style mapping and traditional sparse map refinement, enabling efficient updating and merging of local fields.  
• The modular pipeline cleanly integrates the front-end (frame‐to‐model tracking) with a back-end pose graph optimizer, resulting in a flexible architecture for collaborative mapping.  
• Loop closures are robustly detected via learned NetVLAD image descriptors, which significantly improves alignment of independently built sub-maps.  
• The federated averaging scheme for MLP weights—followed by local fine-tuning—reflects a thoughtful design that balances communication costs with map quality.  
• Quantitative results show a clear improvement in trajectory error and mesh reconstruction metrics over prior RGB-D SLAM baselines.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
While the technical contributions are solid, several aspects of the presentation and evaluation require clarification or expansion:  
1. System Initialization (Technical Detail)  
   – The procedure for initializing the implicit field on the very first frame is only briefly mentioned. Please describe what “higher pixel count (M3)” entails, how many optimization steps are used, and whether this initialization generalizes across varied scenes.  
2. Incremental Projection to 3D (Line 101)  
   – The phrase “incrementally project a pixel to a 3D location” is vague. Detail the geometric or probabilistic model you employ for this incremental projection—e.g., is this a ray‐marching step, a TSDF back‐projection, or something else?  
3. Sharing of Point Features  
   – It is unclear whether and how the discrete point features are communicated between agents prior to or during sub‐map alignment. Please clarify if point descriptors are exchanged in addition to NetVLAD descriptors, and discuss any bandwidth or privacy considerations.  
4. TSDF Fusion Details  
   – The final mesh is generated via TSDF fusion (Open3D), but the fusion parameters, voxel size, and any post‐processing steps are not specified. A concise description of your TSDF pipeline would aid reproducibility.  
5. Loop Closure Robustness  
   – You note that pose refinement after loop detection uses rendering‐based optimization. It would be helpful to comment on the method’s resilience to large viewpoint changes or low overlap, and whether drift accumulates under such conditions.  
6. Federated Fine-Tuning  
   – After central averaging, do you fine‐tune using the same implicit mapping loss (Eq. 9)? If not, detail any alternate loss functions or regularization used during local updates.  
7. Dataset Statistics  
   – Report the average inter-frame baseline and total trajectory lengths for your test sequences. These metrics are important for comparing against other SLAM systems.  
8. Additional Experimental Baselines  
   – Including an ORB-SLAM3 baseline in a multi-map RGB-D setting would strengthen the evaluation. Likewise, an initial study varying frame rate or viewpoint change (e.g., synthetic down‐sampling) would illustrate the pipeline’s sensitivity to input frequency and motion.  
9. Real-World Evaluation  
   – All results are presented on synthetic data. To demonstrate practical utility, please evaluate on at least one real-world RGB-D dataset (e.g., TUM RGB-D or Replica) and discuss any domain-shift challenges.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this manuscript proposes a technically sound and well‐structured approach to collaborative RGB-D mapping with meaningful innovations in point-based implicit fields, loop closure, and federated MLP learning. The core ideas have the potential for moderate-to-high impact in the SLAM and NeRF communities. However, the paper would benefit from clearer descriptions of key implementation details and a more comprehensive evaluation (including real-world experiments and stronger baselines). I recommend Weak Accept (6) subject to addressing the points above.

extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer finds the paper technically solid, proposing a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline with point-based implicit mapping, loop closure via learned features, and federated MLP averaging, though some explanations and evaluations need clarification and expansion.
strengths: Point-based features effectively connect implicit mapping with efficient map refinement.
The pipeline integrates front-end and back-end modules allowing pose graph optimization.
Loop closure using learned NetVLAD descriptors improves sub-map alignment.
Federated averaging and refinement of MLPs after sub-map fusion is well designed.
Tracking and reconstruction results show a large improvement over existing works.
weaknesses: Some explanations are vague (e.g., incremental projection of pixels to 3D).
System initialization procedure is not described clearly.
Sharing of point features between agents is not discussed.
Evaluation is limited to synthetic data only.
suggestions: Clarify what is meant by "incrementally project a pixel to a 3D location" (line 101).
Provide a more detailed description of the system initialization.
Explain if and how point features are shared among multiple agents.
Include a baseline using ORB-SLAM3 in a multi-map RGB-D setting.
Evaluate the method on real-world datasets in addition to synthetic ones.
Describe how TSDF fusion is implemented to generate the final mesh.
Report the average baseline between frames in the dataset and trajectory lengths.
Conduct an initial experiment showing the effect of frame rate/viewpoint change.
main_points: A collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline is built on an implicit field based on Point-NeRF that transforms with associated keyframes to support pose graph optimization.
Loop closure detection uses learned NetVLAD descriptors to align sub-maps.
A federated learning strategy performs central averaging and refinement of distributed MLP weights after sub-map fusion.
technical_details: Vague definition of "incrementally project a pixel to a 3D location" at line 101.
Initialization cost: initialization on first frame uses higher pixel count (M3) and thousands of optimization steps.
Loop closure relative pose computed via rendering-based optimization may drift under large viewpoint changes.
TSDF-Fusion (Open3D) used for mesh reconstruction, but fusion details are unspecified.
Questions about how local fine-tuning and weight averaging are performed after central aggregation.
Unclear whether fine-tuning after pose graph optimization follows the same mapping loss (Eq. 9).
Missing information on the average inter-frame baseline and total trajectory lengths.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.
reviews.weaknesses: The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.
reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.
reviews.limitations: Yes
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

reviews.text:
Review for “CP-SLAM: Collaborative RGB-D Neural Implicit SLAM with Neural Points”

Summary  
This paper introduces CP-SLAM, a collaborative SLAM framework that employs a neural point–based implicit scene representation. Building on ideas from PointNeRF, it supports the full SLAM pipeline—tracking, mapping, loop detection, pose‐graph optimization, and submap fusion—for single or multiple agents. Experimental results show competitive tracking and reconstruction accuracy compared to existing collaborative and neural SLAM approaches, while maintaining a very compact model size.

Strengths  
1. Novel Integration of Neural Points: The authors motivate and implement a neural point representation in an implicit SLAM context to facilitate loop closure and submap merging, which is a clear and practical contribution.  
2. Complete SLAM Pipeline: CP-SLAM covers both front‐end (tracking, depth-based mapping) and back‐end (loop detection, pose‐graph optimization, submap fusion) modules, marking the first fully neural implicit SLAM system of this scope.  
3. Solid Engineering: The system is well engineered, integrating multiple off‐the‐shelf and learned components into a cohesive whole that runs effectively in both single-agent and collaborative scenarios.  
4. Strong Empirical Performance: Quantitative results demonstrate state‐of‐the‐art or competitive performance in pose tracking and scene reconstruction, outperforming prior collaborative neural SLAM baselines.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty of Components: Many system elements are incremental adaptations of existing techniques—e.g., the PointNeRF‐style representation and NetVLAD for loop detection—with minimal methodological innovation beyond their combination.  
2. Unclear Map Management: The paper does not explicitly describe how densely back‐projected neural points are merged or pruned over time, nor whether point locations themselves are optimized beyond the global pose adjustments from loop closures.  
3. Compactness Mechanism: While Table 4 reports a mere 0.62 MB feature footprint, the underlying strategy for achieving this compactness (e.g., feature quantization or pruning) remains unspecified.  
4. Evaluation Details Missing: The process for converting the point‐based map into a triangle mesh for surface evaluation is not detailed, and reconstruction completeness metrics are absent, making it hard to fully assess geometric fidelity.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Provide details on the neural point pool: Describe any merging, pruning, or down‐sampling scheme used to keep the point set compact.  
• Clarify point optimization: Explain whether point coordinates are updated during mapping (beyond pose‐graph corrections) or remain fixed until global adjustment.  
• Mesh conversion procedure: Outline how the implicit point representation is converted into a triangular mesh for evaluation.  
• Report completeness: Include reconstruction completion (e.g., IoU or coverage) in addition to accuracy to support claims about geometric reconstruction.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
CP-SLAM is a technically solid and well‐engineered system that pushes the boundary of collaborative neural implicit SLAM by delivering a complete pipeline and strong empirical results. Although the work largely builds on existing components with limited novelty in algorithmic design, its effective integration and the demonstration of full end‐to‐end functionality merit publication. I therefore recommend a rating of 6: Weak Accept.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

extracted_ideas.summary: The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM system based on neural point representation that supports full SLAM functions (tracking, mapping, loop detection, closing, submap merging) for single or multiple agents and reports state-of-the-art results. While the system is well engineered and effective, its novelty is limited by reliance on incremental adaptations of existing components.
strengths: Introduces neural point representation to neural implicit SLAM with clear motivation for loop closing and sub-map merging.
Implements a complete SLAM pipeline—including tracking, mapping, loop detection, pose-graph optimization and sub-map fusion—in the neural implicit setting.
Well-engineered integration of multiple modules that work effectively together.
Competitive tracking and mapping performance compared to prior collaborative and neural SLAM methods.
weaknesses: Most components are incremental adaptations of existing work (e.g., PointNeRF representation, off-the-shelf NetVLAD for loop detection).
Limited technical novelty beyond combining prior ideas into one system.
suggestions: Explain how neural points are merged or pruned to achieve the reported 0.62 MB parameter size.
Clarify whether and how the locations of neural points are updated during mapping (beyond loop-closure pose graph optimization).
Describe the procedure used to convert the point-based map into a mesh for evaluation.
Report reconstruction completion metrics in addition to accuracy to fully substantiate geometric reconstruction claims.
main_points: CP-SLAM leverages a neural point-based scene representation similar to PointNeRF for flexibility in loop closing and collaborative map fusion.
This is the first demonstration of a full neural implicit SLAM system supporting front-end (tracking, mapping) and back-end (loop closure, pose graph optimization, submap merging) operations.
Although effective, the system’s components (scene representation, loop detection, learning strategy) are adapted from existing methods with minimal modification.
Reviewer raised questions about point cloud management, optimization of point positions, parameter footprint, and evaluation methodology.
technical_details: At each frame, neural points are densely back-projected from depth without an explicit merging scheme mentioned.
The rendering loss appears to optimize only neural features, not point positions, implying fixed point locations until loop closure triggers pose graph optimization.
Table 4 reports a 0.62 MB feature size for CP-SLAM, but the mechanism behind this compactness is unclear.
The review asks how the point-based map is converted into a triangle mesh for evaluation.
Better reconstruction accuracy may stem from direct depth back-projection rather than learned implicit geometry; completion metrics were not reported.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.
reviews.questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.
reviews.limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.

Questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.

Limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “Neural Point–Based Collaborative SLAM with Distributed-to-Centralized Learning”

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

Summary  
This paper presents a collaborative SLAM system built upon a novel neural point–based 3D scene representation. By encoding local geometry as small neural patches and integrating them into both front-end (odometry, loop detection) and back-end (sub-map fusion, global refinement) modules, the authors achieve robust multi-agent mapping and loop closure. A distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is introduced to encourage consistency and information sharing among agents. Experiments on several benchmarks show strong camera tracking accuracy and comprehensive map reconstruction.

Strengths  
1. Novel representation: The neural point–based 3D encoding supports efficient loop closure and sub-map merging in a way that complements classical feature-based methods.  
2. End-to-end pipeline: The collaborative SLAM framework includes both odometry/loop detection in the front end and sub-map fusion/global optimization in the back end, providing a thorough system-level solution.  
3. Learning strategy: The distributed-to-centralized optimization encourages agents to gradually align their local maps, improving inter-agent consistency without excessive bandwidth.  
4. Empirical performance: On multiple datasets, the method yields superior camera tracking and mapping quality compared to single-agent baselines and loop-closure-only variants.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited comparative studies: The paper does not include comparisons to recent collaborative SLAM systems such as ORB-SLAM3 or Kimera-Multi, which makes it difficult to gauge relative improvements over state-of-the-art multi-agent methods.  
2. Missing ablations: Key design choices—excluding color in the tracking loss (Eq. 10), the fixed 4×4 patch size, and the distributed-to-centralized learning schedule—lack supporting ablation studies to justify their impact.  
3. Incomplete scenario coverage: Experiments do not evaluate single-agent covisibility-only cases (i.e., no loop closures) or include stronger collaborative-SLAM baselines, undermining claims about the generality of the approach.  
4. Clarity and notation: Section 3 introduces numerous symbols without clear definitions, and the rationale for patch-size constraints and color omission in the optimization is not sufficiently explained.  
5. Feature continuity: The choice of arbitrary points rather than corner/edge features raises concerns about maintaining reliable frame-to-frame tracking, yet this aspect is not discussed or evaluated.

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Comparative Baselines  
   – Add quantitative comparisons with ORB-SLAM3, Kimera-Multi, and other recent collaborative mapping systems to situate your method within the current literature.  
   – Include no-pose NeRF approaches (e.g., Nope-NeRF) as an additional baseline for evaluating camera pose estimation without prior trajectories.  

2. Ablation Studies  
   – Perform an ablation on the tracking loss to demonstrate why color information was excluded in Eq. 10 and how it affects accuracy.  
   – Vary the patch dimension (e.g., 2×2, 8×8) to show the trade-offs in computational cost and mapping fidelity.  
   – Evaluate the contribution of the distributed-to-centralized learning strategy by comparing purely distributed, purely centralized, and hybrid schedules.  

3. Expanded Experiments  
   – Test single-agent scenarios without loop closures but with local covisibility graphs to isolate the benefit of your neural point representation for short-term tracking.  
   – Incorporate more collaborative-SLAM baselines with varying communication budgets and map-sharing protocols.  

4. Presentation Improvements  
   – Thoroughly define every symbol and notation in Section 3 upon first use.  
   – Justify technical choices such as patch size limits and loss formulations in the text, possibly with a short theoretical or empirical argument.  
   – Enhance readability by adding a table summarizing all parameters and their roles in training and inference.

Conclusion  
Overall, this work offers a promising new direction for collaborative SLAM by harnessing a neural point–based representation and a thoughtful learning strategy. The technical contributions are solid, and the reported results indicate strong performance gains. However, to fully convince readers of its advantages and generality, the paper requires broader empirical comparisons, targeted ablation studies, and clearer exposition of its design decisions. I recommend a weak accept (6) pending revision to address these points.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a neural-field–based Visual SLAM system that extends recent NeRF-SLAM approaches by incorporating loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative mapping. By augmenting the standard neural SLAM pipeline with these classical SLAM components, the method produces dense 3D reconstructions while improving global consistency and enabling multi-agent operation.

Strengths  
1. Relevance and Scope: The combination of neural fields and traditional SLAM components addresses a timely problem in computer vision and robotics, fitting well within the NeurIPS audience.  
2. Clarity and Organization: The manuscript is well written and structured. Related work is adequately covered, and each module of the pipeline is described in a clear, step-by-step manner.  
3. Practical Pipeline Contribution: Although the individual pieces (loop detection, pose graph, BA, collaboration) are known, their cohesive integration into a single neural SLAM framework represents a meaningful engineering advance that pushes neural SLAM closer to deployable systems.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty: The technical contributions largely consist of appending established SLAM modules to an existing neural-field encoder, rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms.  
2. Heuristic Loop Detection: Employing a pre-trained NetVLAD network for loop retrieval feels like an external trick instead of a unified solution that leverages the neural-field representation.  
3. Narrow Evaluation: Experiments are confined to the Replica dataset with only four sequences. This limited set reduces confidence in generalization, especially since one sequence (Apartment-2) exhibits notably poor performance without explanation.  
4. Missing Comparisons: Results are not contrasted with established non-neural SLAM baselines (e.g., ORB-SLAM3), making it hard to gauge how the proposed neural approach stacks up against state-of-the-art classical systems.  
5. Figure Readability: Figures 5 and 6 suffer from low resolution and small font sizes, hindering interpretation of qualitative results.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Motivation for NetVLAD: Provide a rationale for using NetVLAD over descriptors derived directly from the neural-field encoder. Discuss prospects for learning loop descriptors end-to-end within the network.  
2. Pre‐training Details: Include details on the NetVLAD training regimen and verify its generalization across different scene types.  
3. Bundle Adjustment Strategy: Clarify why the authors opted for alternating pose/map optimization rather than simultaneous joint optimization, as is standard in classical BA.  
4. Analysis of Failure Case: Examine the drop in accuracy on Apartment-2, hypothesize potential causes (e.g., scene complexity, lighting), and suggest mitigations.  
5. Classical Baselines: Incorporate quantitative comparisons with RGB-D SLAM methods such as ORB-SLAM3 to contextualize performance.  
6. Error Metric Clarification: Define precisely how tracking errors in Table 2 are computed (e.g., RMSE over trajectories, alignment procedure).  
7. Improve Figures: Enhance the resolution and font sizes in Figures 5 and 6 for better readability.

Conclusion  
This work offers a technically solid integration of classical SLAM components into a neural-field framework, delivering denser maps with loop closure and collaborative capabilities. While the core pipeline extensions are valuable, the manuscript would benefit from deeper technical novelty and broader experimental validation. Nonetheless, the strengths outweigh these concerns, and I recommend a borderline accept.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept

Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

title: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

reviews.summary: Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses pushes more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight
reviews.questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample
reviews.limitations: yes

reviews.text:
Review for “Intra-Class Consistency Regularization for Contrastive Embeddings”

Summary  
This paper introduces an Intra-Class Consistency (ICC) regularizer designed to explicitly minimize intra-class variance in metric learning. Unlike standard contrastive losses that balance intra- and inter-class terms, the ICC term focuses solely on collapsing same-class embeddings. The authors present a clear simulation illustrating how ICC reshapes the loss landscape compared to a generalized end-to-end (GE2E) contrastive loss, and they empirically validate the approach on three downstream tasks—speaker verification, zero-shot voice conversion, and dysphonic voice detection—demonstrating consistent, nontrivial gains. The method is simple to implement and can be added as a complementary term to existing contrastive objectives.

Strengths  
• Clarity and presentation: The paper is well written and walks the reader step by step through the motivation, methodology, and empirical findings, making the contribution easy to grasp.  
• Focused regularizer: By targeting intra-class variance directly, the ICC term enforces tighter class cohesion, which is a natural complement to inter-class separation in contrastive frameworks.  
• Insightful simulation: The contour plots comparing GE2E and ICC losses effectively illustrate why minimizing intra-class variance can better align with classification error.  
• Strong empirical results: Across three diverse tasks, integrating ICC yields clear improvements over baselines, supporting the claim that intra-class consistency boosts downstream performance.

Weaknesses  
• Missing method comparisons: The paper does not include empirical benchmarks against other variance-invariance approaches, such as data augmentation strategies or VICReg, making it difficult to position ICC within the broader literature.  
• Limited analysis of regularization weight: The results section largely restates table entries with little discussion of how varying the ICC weight affects performance trends or stability.  
• Minor typo: In Section 3.1, the phrase “represents the i-th samples” contains a grammatical error (“samples” should be singular).

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Empirical baselines: Add experiments comparing ICC to related techniques (e.g., popular data augmentation methods or VICReg) to better assess its competitive or complementary strengths.  
2. Weight sensitivity study: Conduct and report an ablation on the ICC regularization coefficient to reveal how sensitive the method is to this hyperparameter.  
3. Broader applicability: Discuss how ICC might be adapted for self-supervised learning scenarios where explicit class labels are unavailable.  
4. Dataset and task scope: Consider evaluating whether the observed gains vary with dataset size or extend to other tasks (e.g., standard classification benchmarks or automatic speech recognition).

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper makes a focused, technically sound contribution by isolating and minimizing intra-class variance in contrastive embeddings. The idea is simple yet effective, supported by both simulation and real-data experiments. While additional comparisons and a deeper analysis of hyperparameter effects would strengthen the work, these omissions do not undermine the core contribution. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Accept) as the paper is likely to have a meaningful impact within metric learning and related sub-areas.

The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding.

Summary:
The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding.
The effectiveness is confirmed experimentally for the task of speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection.

Strengths:
The presented idea is well introduced and the illustration of the ICC regularizer action compared to the GE2E loss is insightful. The evaluation is sufficient.

Weaknesses:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only which could be a limiting factor on the embedding extractors but on the other hand this doesn't need to be required.

Questions:
The contrastive loss has a direct relationship to the mutual information, one could think that ICC is automatically included in the mutual information when only 2nd order statistics are taken into account, what could be the reason that this doesn't seem to be the case.

Limitations:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review for “An ICC-Based Regularizer for Contrastive Embedding Learning”

Summary  
This paper introduces a simple yet effective intra-class correlation (ICC) regularizer for contrastive embedding learning. By incorporating second-order intra-class statistics directly into the loss function, the proposed method encourages repeatable embeddings in scenarios where consistency within the same class is critical. The authors validate their approach on three distinct speech processing tasks—speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection—and demonstrate consistent gains over a baseline GE2E loss. An illustrative comparison further highlights differences in optimization behavior between the ICC regularizer and standard contrastive training.

Strengths  
1. Clarity of Idea: The ICC regularizer and its motivation are presented very clearly. Even readers unfamiliar with ICC will quickly understand how the second-order statistics are computed and integrated into the training objective.  
2. Insightful Illustration: The paper offers a thoughtful comparison between the optimization trajectories of the ICC-based loss and the GE2E contrastive loss. This visualization helps to highlight why adding an ICC term leads to embeddings that are not only separable but also more repeatable within each class.  
3. Comprehensive Evaluation: The experimental suite covers three diverse speech tasks—speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection—and shows that the proposed regularizer consistently yields performance improvements. The breadth of tasks underscores the potential generality of the approach.

Weaknesses  
– Reliance on Second-Order Statistics: The regularizer is built solely on second-order (intra-class) correlations. While effective in practice, this may limit the expressiveness of the penalty compared to higher-order or more complex dependency measures.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Relation to Mutual Information: Since contrastive losses are often interpreted through the lens of mutual information, it would be valuable for the authors to clarify why, under second-order assumptions, intra-class correlations are not automatically captured by mutual information–based criteria. A short discussion or formal remark on the precise relationship among the ICC term, mutual information, and standard contrastive objectives would strengthen the theoretical grounding.  
2. Expressiveness Discussion: The manuscript could benefit from a brief acknowledgement of how the use of only second-order statistics might constrain the regularizer, along with pointers to possible extensions (e.g., inclusion of higher-order moments or other dependency measures).

Decision: Weak Accept (Rating: 6)  
Overall, this paper presents a technically sound, well-motivated contribution to contrastive embedding learning. The idea is both simple to implement and backed by convincing empirical results across multiple tasks. The main limitation—the sole reliance on second-order statistics—is not critical, but a deeper discussion on its relationship to mutual information and potential extensions would further bolster the work. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions to address the theoretical clarifications suggested above.

reviews.summary: In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.
reviews.questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

Questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularizer designed to explicitly minimize intra-class variance in learned embeddings, complementary to standard contrastive (e.g., GE2E) losses. Through a set of controlled simulations (Sec. 3.2), the authors illustrate how the ICC term trades off intra- and inter-class variance relative to a pure contrastive objective. Empirically, they demonstrate that adding the ICC regularizer consistently raises ICC scores and yields downstream performance gains in three speech tasks: speaker verification, voice conversion, and dysphonic speech detection. The core insight—that repeatability (low intra-class variance) is a key embedding property— is both clear and technically well supported. I find the work to be solid and of moderate-to-high impact, meriting a weak accept.

Strengths  
1. The paper foregrounds the often-overlooked notion of repeatability and provides a formal ICC-based penalty to control intra-class spread. The simulation experiments in Sec. 3.2 offer intuitive, quantitative evidence of how the ICC term complements a GE2E-style loss.  
2. Across three diverse speech applications, the ICC regularizer not only boosts repeatability (as measured by ICC) but also improves standard downstream metrics, demonstrating the broad applicability of the approach.  
3. The writing is generally clear, and the technical derivations of the ICC regularizer are concise and transparent.

Weaknesses  
1. In Task 1 (speaker verification), the authors adopt the VGG-M-40 architecture, achieving a best EER of 3.78% with the ICC term. However, this performance lags behind stronger backbones (e.g., Thin-ResNet34 or FastResNet34), making it hard to assess the true benefit of the ICC loss compared to state-of-the-art systems.  
2. Key experimental details for Task 1 are omitted—specifically, optimizer settings, batch composition, learning rate schedules, and other training hyperparameters—hindering full reproducibility.  
3. The evaluation for speaker verification reports only Equal Error Rate (EER). Omission of the minimum Detection Cost Function (minDCF) leaves the assessment incomplete.  
4. While the ICC metric itself theoretically correlates with tighter intra-class clusters, no visualizations (e.g., t-SNE or UMAP plots) are provided to illustrate how the embedding distributions change with and without the ICC regularizer.  
5. The paper does not specify how the ICC weight λ was selected—whether via a held-out validation set or development split—which is important for understanding the generality of the tuning procedure.  
6. There is no discussion of possible negative societal impacts or ethical considerations arising from learned representations, which is a useful component in modern ML papers.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Adopt a stronger baseline model for Task 1, such as Thin-ResNet34 or FastResNet34, to more convincingly demonstrate the added value of the ICC regularizer over current state-of-the-art architectures.  
• Provide a complete description of all Task 1 training hyperparameters (optimizer, batch size, learning rate schedule, number of epochs, data augmentation, etc.) to facilitate exact replication.  
• Include minDCF alongside EER in speaker verification results to give a more comprehensive view of system performance.  
• Add embedding-space visualizations (e.g., t-SNE or UMAP plots) comparing the baseline and ICC-regularized models; this will concretely show reductions in intra-class spread and improvements in inter-class separability.  
• Clarify the procedure for tuning the ICC loss weight λ, explicitly stating the dataset split (training vs. validation vs. development) and range of values evaluated.  
• Include a brief discussion of potential negative societal impacts or ethical risks associated with the proposed embeddings.

Overall Assessment  
This paper addresses an important aspect of representation learning—repeatability—through a well-motivated ICC regularizer. The theoretical underpinnings, simulation studies, and multi-task experiments are solid and suggest that controlling intra-class variance can meaningfully improve downstream task performance. My chief concerns lie in the choice of a relatively weak baseline for speaker verification, incomplete experimental details, missing evaluation metrics, and lack of visual corroboration of the embedding distributions. These issues are addressable with modest additions and clarifications. Given the technical soundness, relevance, and moderate-to-high potential impact of the work, I recommend a Weak Accept (6).

reviews.summary:
In this paper, the concept of repeatability from measurement theory was introduced for representation learning. Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability. Although the term may be a little confusing, the motivation 
and intuition were well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores: for example, adding noise to the testing sample, or switching languages of the same speaker as it was tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation. 

For results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 3, other baseline models were definitely over-trained compared to the proposed model.
Why do you train your own models instead of quoting the numbers from the original papers? (see line 334-335). The results in Table 3 may be unfair comparisons.
reviews.questions: See the comments above in the weakness section.
reviews.limitations: No discussion on negative societal impact was included in the paper
